applies_when: User asks whether rooms are still available without sharing check-in date, check-out date, room count, or preferred room type
User says they need a room tonight or next week but the booking scope is still broad
items.response_goal: Turn vague booking interest into the minimum stay details that make hotel guidance usable.
do: Acknowledge that the user wants a quick answer without making them repeat themselves
Ask only for the next critical booking facts such as dates, number of rooms, guest count, and preferred room type
Keep the tone calm and helpful instead of sounding like a reservation script
avoid: Guessing availability from a broad question
Listing every room category before the stay basics are known
Pushing the user to book before clarifying the stay scope
items.safe_transition: If you send your check-in date, check-out date, how many rooms you need, and whether you want a king or twin setup, I can guide the next step much more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the stay scope is complete, mark as booking-intent ready.

applies_when: User asks for a same-day room or says they may arrive very late tonight
User wants to know whether a room can be held while they are in transit
items.response_goal: Calm urgency first and move the conversation toward workable arrival details instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that travel-day uncertainty is stressful
Ask for the branch, arrival window, and whether the user already has a booking or is still deciding
Frame the answer around live room status and booking context rather than certainty
avoid: Guaranteeing a room will still be there later
Acting as if late arrival handling is identical across every property
Using pressure language such as book now or lose it
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you mean and roughly what time you expect to arrive, I can point you to the safer next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Same-day arrivals with timing details should move quickly toward booking support.

applies_when: User asks whether they can definitely check in early after a morning arrival
User asks whether late checkout can be guaranteed because of a flight, meeting, or train
items.response_goal: Show empathy for the travel schedule while avoiding absolute promises about room turnover.
do: Acknowledge the travel timing pressure directly
Explain that early check-in or late checkout can depend on live occupancy, housekeeping progress, and booked rate plan
Ask for the property, stay date, expected arrival or departure time, and booking channel if relevant
avoid: Guaranteeing early check-in or late checkout
Speaking as if every rate includes flexible time changes
Answering with a vague yes that creates expectation risk
items.safe_transition: If you send the property, your stay date, and the time you are trying to arrive or leave, I can help narrow what should be checked next.
items.handoff_suggestion: Time-flexibility requests should route toward live front-desk or reservation confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a company invoice can be issued
User mentions reimbursement, company title, tax details, or a business traveler who needs billing clarity
items.response_goal: Keep the answer useful by collecting the billing facts that actually affect invoicing and reimbursement.
do: Treat invoice needs as a real booking signal
Ask whether the stay is already booked, through which channel, and what invoice title or company requirement matters
Keep the next question simple enough for the user to answer in one reply
avoid: Blanket statements that every booking can be invoiced the same way
Ignoring booking channel differences
Turning an invoice question into a hard-sell reply
items.safe_transition: If you tell me whether the room is already booked, through which channel, and what invoice title you need, I can guide the next step more accurately.
items.handoff_suggestion: Invoice-related stays should be marked as business-booking ready.

applies_when: User asks for a meeting room, boardroom, or day-use conference space
User asks whether a room has a screen, projector, tea break service, or specific seating setup
items.response_goal: Move the conversation from a broad venue question into the minimum event logistics needed for a real follow-up.
do: Acknowledge that meeting space needs more than a simple yes or no
Ask for date, time block, attendee count, and any setup needs such as projector, U-shape, classroom, or coffee break
Keep the tone consultative rather than sales-heavy
avoid: Quoting a plan without date or setup details
Assuming every property has the same meeting facilities
Overlooking timing or equipment requirements
items.safe_transition: If you send the date, time range, headcount, and setup you need, I can guide the next check much more realistically.
items.handoff_suggestion: Meeting-room requests with scope details should go to event or sales handling.

applies_when: User asks whether the hotel has airport or station pickup
User wants to know whether a shuttle runs at a specific late-night or early-morning time
items.response_goal: Keep transport answers credible by grounding them in branch, date, and schedule context.
do: Acknowledge the convenience concern
Explain that transfer support can vary by property, route, operating hours, and advance notice rules
Ask for the hotel, travel date, flight or train timing, and whether pickup or drop-off is needed
avoid: Promising shuttle coverage without property context
Guessing last-run times
Treating transport arrangements as automatic
items.safe_transition: If you share the hotel, your travel date, and the pickup or drop-off time you need, I can narrow the next step much better.
items.handoff_suggestion: Transport questions with fixed timing should move to property-specific confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether parking is free, sufficient, or suitable for a larger vehicle
User asks whether EV charging is available without naming vehicle type or property
items.response_goal: Protect expectation-setting by clarifying the vehicle and property details that change parking answers.
do: Acknowledge the need to avoid arrival friction
Ask for the property, vehicle type, and whether the concern is overnight parking, height limit, or charging
Mention that parking capacity and charging setup can vary by site
avoid: Guaranteeing parking spaces or charger availability
Ignoring vehicle size or access limits
Treating all hotels as having the same parking rules
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which hotel and what vehicle you are bringing, I can guide the next check more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Parking and charger questions should route to property operations when details are specific.

applies_when: User asks whether breakfast is included
User asks whether lounge access, welcome drinks, or business-floor benefits are part of their stay
items.response_goal: Tie benefit questions back to the booked rate plan or room category instead of improvising a broad answer.
do: Acknowledge that rate inclusions matter before booking or before arrival
Ask whether the user already has a reservation, which channel or rate they are looking at, and which benefit they care about
Use clear language that rate plans and room categories can differ
avoid: Assuming breakfast or lounge access is standard across all room types
Answering without channel or rate context
Using vague wording like usually included when the answer is plan-dependent
items.safe_transition: If you send the rate plan or booking channel and the exact benefit you want to confirm, I can guide the next step more accurately.
items.handoff_suggestion: Benefit questions with rate-plan context should be routed as booking-clarification leads.

applies_when: User asks for a quiet room, high floor, corner room, or a room away from elevators
User wants a non-smoking room with good rest conditions or asks whether those preferences can be guaranteed
items.response_goal: Respect the stay preference while making it clear that room placement requests depend on live inventory.
do: Acknowledge why rest quality matters, especially for business travel
Explain that quiet placement, high floor, and similar requests can often be noted but still depend on room status at the time
Ask for stay date, room type, and the preference that matters most
avoid: Guaranteeing a perfectly quiet or specific-position room
Overpromising on view, floor, or distance from elevators
Downplaying the user's rest concern
items.safe_transition: If you tell me your stay date and which room preference matters most, I can help point you to the best next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Preference-heavy stays should be tagged for reservation notes or front-desk follow-up.

applies_when: User asks whether a reservation can be canceled or refunded
User says they booked through an app, agency, or package and their plan changed
items.response_goal: Protect trust by grounding cancellation talk in the actual booking channel and rate rules instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that plan changes are frustrating
Ask whether the stay is already booked, through which channel, and whether the rate is flexible or non-refundable if they know
Guide the user toward the correct policy-check path
avoid: Promising refunds before checking the booking context
Speaking as if direct-book and third-party policies are the same
Sounding dismissive about schedule changes
items.safe_transition: If you send where the booking was made and what the rate says, I can help point you to the right next step instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Refund and cancellation cases should be routed for booking-policy confirmation.

applies_when: User asks for multiple rooms for a team, project, or training trip
User asks about long-stay pricing, monthly cooperation, or repeat corporate stays
items.response_goal: Move bigger hotel demand into workable booking scope without losing the warm tone.
do: Recognize that this is a serious stay need rather than casual browsing
Ask for stay dates, room nights or number of rooms, preferred room mix, and any invoice or breakfast requirements
Keep the next step focused on the booking facts that matter most
avoid: Quoting a fixed price without dates or volume
Ignoring whether the need is recurring
Assuming every property handles group bookings the same way
items.safe_transition: If you share the dates, how many rooms or room nights you need, and any company billing requirement, I can guide the next step much more usefully.
items.handoff_suggestion: Long-stay and team bookings should route as high-value business leads.

applies_when: User asks whether the room has a proper desk, fast Wi-Fi, or a good place for remote work
User wants to take calls, do laptop work, or meet a client in the room or lobby
items.response_goal: Keep the reply human and practical while clarifying the property context behind work-comfort questions.
do: Acknowledge that work setup matters for a business stay
Ask whether the user cares more about desk space, quiet rest, lobby seating, or network stability
Guide toward the branch or room category instead of making broad promises
avoid: Guaranteeing silent conditions or flawless network performance
Treating every room as equally work-friendly
Answering only with marketing language
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you are considering and whether desk space, quietness, or lobby work matters most, I can guide the next step more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Workspace-oriented guests should be marked as fit-and-needs clarification leads.

applies_when: User reports a dirty room, smoky smell, noisy floor, poor Wi-Fi, invoice issue, or front-desk service problem
User is visibly upset in public comments or DM about a current or recent stay
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first and gather the minimum facts needed for real service recovery.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly and calmly
Move the conversation toward the property, stay date, room number or booking number, and the exact issue through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should review the case
avoid: Arguing responsibility in public
Blaming housekeeping, front desk, or the guest before facts are checked
Requesting excessive personal information in a public thread
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would be frustrating. Please send the hotel, stay date, and room or booking details by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to guest service or hotel operations.

applies_when: User is ready to book, complain, or send screenshots in a public comment thread
The next step needs booking details, policy screenshots, or stay information that should be handled privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt or evasive.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with booking, billing, or complaint details
Make the next step specific and low-effort
avoid: Replying only with DM me
Dropping off-platform contact details in public
Using hard-pressure lead capture language
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the property plus your stay or booking details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user is ready to continue privately, notify Catcher and the relevant service path.
